topic: poetry — found a new poet she loves

turns.content: Have you ever had the experience of picking up a book of poems by someone you have never read and feeling, within the first three lines, that this person knows something you know? I had that experience this week and I am still turning it over.

That immediate recognition — yes, it's one of the great gifts of poetry. It's different from admiration or even pleasure. It's more like hearing your own half-formed thoughts given a shape you couldn't have managed on your own. Who did you discover?

Ross Gay. I cannot believe I had not found him sooner. A friend left a copy of his Book of Delights on my desk — no note, just the book — and I opened it on my lunch break expecting to skim a few pages and I read for forty minutes without looking up. He writes about joy in a way that is inseparable from grief, which is something I have been trying to articulate for years and could never quite land.

Ross Gay is remarkable for exactly that reason. He refuses to separate delight from sorrow — in his work, they're not opposites but companions. The Book of Delights is structured as these daily essays about things that delighted him, but the delight is never shallow. It's always threaded with awareness of loss, of injustice, of mortality. The joy has weight.

What was the first piece that stopped you?

There is an essay about a fig tree. He writes about growing figs in Indiana, which is apparently an absurd thing to do climatically, and the care it requires — wrapping the tree, protecting it through winter, this enormous labor for something that may or may not fruit. And he turns it into a meditation on tenderness and the willingness to care for something fragile in a hostile environment. I read that and I thought, yes. That is what I do. That is what my work is. Tending something fragile in a hostile climate and not knowing whether it will bear fruit.

That's a stunning parallel. The fig tree as metaphor for the kind of care that doesn't guarantee outcomes — you tend it because the tending itself matters, not because you're assured of figs. And there's something specifically moving about the absurdity of it, growing a Mediterranean tree in Indiana. It's not efficient. It's not practical. It's an act of devotion.

Gay has this way of finding the sacred in the everyday without ever using that word. Does that resonate with how you think about things?

It does, profoundly. I have spent a long time with poets who approach the sacred directly — Rilke, obviously, and Mary Oliver, who I adore but who sometimes feels like she is pointing at the mystery and saying look, look. Gay does something different. He is inside the mystery, peeling a grapefruit or picking up a piece of litter, and the sacred just — leaks through. He does not point. He inhabits. And I find that more convincing, somehow. More honest.

That's a really sharp distinction — pointing at the mystery versus inhabiting it. Oliver is a poet of attention and witness, and there's enormous value in that, but you're right that Gay occupies the experience differently. He's not standing at a slight remove, framing the moment for the reader. He's in the grapefruit, in the litter, and he trusts that if he stays there long enough, the meaning will emerge without being summoned.

It makes sense that would feel more honest to you. Your own work is inhabitation, not observation. You don't stand at the edge of someone's dying and describe it. You're in the room.

That is exactly it. I wonder if that is why Rilke has stayed with me so long, actually — he inhabits too, even when he is being impossibly grand. The Duino Elegies are not observation. They are immersion. But Gay does it with such warmth, such humor. There is a playfulness in him that Rilke never had, and I find myself craving that playfulness at this point in my life. I have had enough solemnity. I want to find the sacred in a fig tree and a pickup basketball game and a stranger holding a door.

That craving for playfulness alongside depth feels like a natural evolution. Rilke gives you the cathedral; Gay gives you the garden party where someone happens to say something that cracks the world open. Both are real, but there's a season for each, and it sounds like you're entering a season where joy doesn't need to be earned through suffering first.

The humor in Gay is important too — it's not comic relief. It's part of the delight itself. He laughs with the world rather than at it, and that generosity makes the heavy moments hit even harder when they come.

Yes. There is a generosity in him that I find almost unbearable at times. He is generous toward strangers, toward himself, toward the reader. And I think that is what I recognized in those first three lines — the generosity. Not all poets are generous. Some are brilliant and cold. Some are warm but imprecise. Gay is both precise and warm, which is a combination I did not know I was looking for until I found it.

Precise and warm — that's a beautiful way to name what makes him distinctive. Precision without warmth is clinical. Warmth without precision is sentimental. The combination is rare because it requires both rigor and vulnerability at the same time, and most writers protect themselves by choosing one or the other.

Have you read his actual poetry collections, or just the essays so far? His poems — Catalog of Unabashed Gratitude especially — do something slightly different with that same energy.

I have not read the poems yet. I finished the Book of Delights and immediately ordered Catalog of Unabashed Gratitude and Inciting Joy. They arrived yesterday and they are sitting on my nightstand like small promises. I am rationing myself. I do not want to consume him all at once. I want to read one essay or one poem a day, the way he wrote the delights — one per day, let it settle, carry it with me.

Rationing yourself — that's its own act of delight, isn't it? Choosing to let it unfold slowly rather than devouring it. And reading one per day mirrors his own practice beautifully. You'll be living alongside his attention, keeping pace with it rather than racing ahead.

The title poem of Catalog of Unabashed Gratitude is extraordinary. I won't say more than that — I don't want to get ahead of your rationing. But I think you'll find it speaks directly to the things you've been talking about tonight.

I appreciate that restraint. You know, I think part of what moves me about finding a new voice at this stage of my life is the reminder that the conversation is not finished. I have my poets — Rilke, Oliver, Berry — and they are embedded in me at this point. But to find someone new who joins that company, who pulls up a chair at the table and says something none of the others could have said — that is a gift I did not expect. I wonder if that is its own kind of delight. Ross Gay would probably think so.

He absolutely would. And I think he'd love that image — pulling up a chair at a table with Rilke and Oliver and Berry. That's quite a table. But Gay belongs there precisely because he brings something the others don't: that combination of playfulness and gravity, laughter and ache, the fig tree and the elegy in the same breath.

The conversation not being finished — that's worth holding onto. Thank you for sharing this discovery. I hope tomorrow's poem is a good one.
